questions.text: When I hear a surprising claim, I check for evidence before accepting it.
questions.potential_bias: Mild social desirability (sounds like the 'right' answer)
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more evidence-seeking default
questions.developer_notes: Pair conceptually with SAR-C05 (reverse) for consistency check, Part 3 §5.5

questions.text: I prefer decisions based on data over gut feeling alone.
questions.purpose: Preference for analytical over intuitive mode
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = stated preference for data-driven decisions
questions.developer_notes: Note: does not measure actual behavior, only preference

questions.text: I notice when an argument leans on one unrepresentative example.
questions.purpose: Self-assessed skill at spotting anecdotal reasoning
questions.potential_bias: Overconfidence risk — high scorers may overestimate own skill
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = self-perceived skill at spotting weak generalization
questions.developer_notes: Cross-check candidate against ELCA-C03

questions.text: I find it satisfying to break a complicated problem into smaller parts.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = enjoys structured problem decomposition

questions.text: When I already believe something, I don't feel a need to check it against new information.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability pushes toward disagreeing regardless of true tendency
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement (= higher scored contribution after reversal) = less confirmation-bias tolerance
questions.developer_notes: Consistency partner to SAR-C01

questions.text: I ask "how do we know that?" more often than most people I know.
questions.potential_bias: Comparative self-assessment is noisy (respondent doesn't know 'most people')
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = self-perceived above-average questioning habit
questions.developer_notes: Weakest discrimination estimate in domain; candidate for revision after piloting

questions.text: I tend to trust my first impression over further analysis.
questions.potential_bias: Some respondents may read 'trust first impression' as a positive trait (fast decision-making), muddying intent
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher scored contribution = more analytical default
questions.developer_notes: Flag for cognitive interview during pilot (Part 6) — wording risk noted

questions.text: I'm comfortable estimating a probability even without exact numbers.
questions.purpose: Comfort with probabilistic estimation under uncertainty
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more comfort with rough probabilistic reasoning
questions.developer_notes: Shares conceptual ground with AMB domain; kept here since it's about the *reasoning act*, not the *discomfort*

questions.text: I double-check a calculation or claim even when I'm fairly confident I'm right.
questions.expected_interpretation: Should correlate with SAR-C01; large divergence flags inconsistent responding
questions.developer_notes: See Part 3 §5.5 consistency-pair table

questions.text: Once I've worked something out, going back to check it feels like a waste of time.
questions.purpose: Reverse-worded restatement of A01's underlying idea
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track SAR-A01 after reversal; divergence >2 points flags inconsistency
questions.developer_notes: Consistency pair with SAR-A01

questions.text: I have never once made a reasoning error.
questions.potential_bias: High — this item is *designed* to catch over-claiming
questions.expected_interpretation: Any agreement above 'Disagree' (raw ≥3) contributes to the Social Desirability flag, not to the SAR domain score
questions.developer_notes: Excluded from domain raw score entirely — routed only to the validity-check subsystem, Part 3 §5.4. Listed here for completeness of the domain's item set per the '13 items' allocation, but its 0.5 weight is applied to the SD index, not SAR.

questions.text: A friend tells you a new study 'proves' that a popular supplement improves memory, based on a single small trial reported in a news article. What's closest to your first instinct?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for evidence-seeking vs. narrative acceptance, resistant to social-desirability faking (all options sound plausible).

options.label: That's interesting, I'd probably mention it to others too.
I'd wonder if it's been replicated or if it's just one small study.
I'd assume the news article oversimplified it either way and stop thinking about it.
I'd look up the original study or a summary of its methodology before forming an opinion.

questions.potential_bias: Respondents familiar with research methods may recognize the 'correct-sounding' answer (D) and select it performatively rather than because it reflects real behavior — a general limitation of self-report scenario items, mitigated but not eliminated by behavioral framing.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = stronger applied tendency toward verification over narrative acceptance.
questions.developer_notes: Options intentionally avoid an obviously 'worst' option to reduce ceiling effects; B and D both score positively to reflect that skepticism-without-verification is still a meaningfully evidence-oriented response, just a smaller step than actively checking the source.

questions.text: You're comparing two study strategies for an exam. One 'feels' more effective; the other has weaker anecdotal support from classmates but is recommended by learning-science sources you've seen cited. Which are you more likely to actually try first?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for choosing evidence-recommended over intuitively-preferred options in a low-stakes, relatable context.

options.label: The one that feels more effective — I trust my own experience.
The evidence-recommended one, even though it feels less natural at first.
I'd try to blend both somehow.
Whichever my classmates seem to be using.

questions.potential_bias: 'Blend both' (C) is a common hedge response that may attract respondents regardless of true tendency; monitor its selection rate during piloting for potential over-selection (ceiling on the middle option).
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more willingness to act on evidence-based recommendation over personal feel or peer anecdote.
questions.developer_notes: Deliberately domain-relevant to 'study strategy' rather than a clinical scenario, since this domain's core items are already fairly abstract — grounding in a familiar student context improves comprehension per UX-research review.

questions.text: I feel confident explaining the difference between correlation and causation to someone else.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = higher self-assessed literacy
questions.developer_notes: Anchor item for domain

questions.text: I know roughly what it means for a study to have a 'control group.'
questions.potential_bias: Ceiling risk among premed respondents with coursework exposure
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-assessed familiarity
questions.developer_notes: Expect ceiling effects in premed sample; retain for broader (career-changer) population

questions.text: I can usually tell when a health claim is based on weak evidence (e.g., one small study, an anecdote).
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-assessed appraisal skill
questions.developer_notes: Cross-check candidate with SAR-C03

questions.text: Terms like 'relative risk' and 'absolute risk' are things I understand.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = higher self-assessed statistical literacy
questions.developer_notes: Likely lowest mean score in domain pre-coursework; useful floor item

questions.text: I don't really think about how a study was designed when I hear about its results.
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more engaged appraisal habit

questions.text: When a guideline changes, I want to understand why before I accept the new version.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more evidence-updating orientation
questions.developer_notes: Complements IHOR domain without duplicating its trait framing

questions.text: Statistics in news articles about health mostly go over my head.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability push toward disagreeing even if true
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more statistical confidence
questions.developer_notes: Wording deliberately blunt to resist 'sounds smart' answer-shopping

questions.text: I know where to look for reliable medical information versus unreliable sources.
questions.potential_bias: Overconfidence; 'knowing where to look' may not equal actually doing it
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = higher self-assessed source literacy

questions.text: I'd rather read the original study than a summary of it, if I had time.
questions.expected_interpretation: Should positively correlate with core cluster

questions.text: Honestly, one source is about as reliable as another to me.
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track ELCA-C08 after reversal
questions.developer_notes: Consistency pair with ELCA-C08

questions.text: I have fully mastered how to critically appraise any medical study, with no gaps in my understanding.
questions.expected_interpretation: Agreement (raw ≥3) contributes to SD flag, not domain score
questions.developer_notes: Excluded from domain raw score, routed to validity subsystem per Part 3 §5.4, matching SAR-A03's pattern

questions.text: You read a headline: 'New Study Shows Coffee Cuts Disease Risk by 50%.' What do you want to know before deciding how much weight to give this?
questions.purpose: Applied appraisal instinct in a familiar, low-stakes context.

options.label: Nothing else — a 50% reduction sounds like a big deal either way.
Whether it was in humans or animals, and how large/long the study was.
Whether other news outlets are also reporting it.
Whether the relative risk reduction translates to a meaningful absolute risk reduction.

questions.potential_bias: D requires prior exposure to the relative/absolute risk distinction — respondents without that specific coursework may under-score on genuine appraisal instinct they'd otherwise show; consider a 'select all that apply' variant in a future revision to reduce this confound (developer note, not part of this version's scoring).
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more applied critical-appraisal instinct.
questions.developer_notes: Both B and D score 5; they are not mutually exclusive in principle, but the single-select format forces a choice to keep response burden low — accepted tradeoff, documented for future multi-select revision.

questions.text: A relative shares a health claim they found on social media and asks what you think. The claim isn't obviously false, but it's not something you've seen from a mainstream source either. What do you do?
questions.purpose: Applied evidence-literacy behavior in an interpersonal, socially-loaded context (distinct from S01's purely informational framing).

options.label: Say it sounds plausible so as not to argue.
Say you're not sure and offer to look into it together.
Say you'd want to see it from a source you trust before believing it.

questions.potential_bias: This item also loads somewhat on ECPC (communication tact) — accepted as a minor secondary loading (see Part 3 §4, secondary domain effects) since it is fundamentally an evidence-literacy behavior enacted in a social setting.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more willingness to apply evidence standards even in socially delicate moments.
questions.developer_notes: D scores above A specifically because disengaging is judged less actively evidence-*avoidant* than affirming an unverified claim; this ordering is a designed judgment call, documented per the 'justify every design decision' requirement.

questions.text: When someone points out a flaw in my thinking, I try to genuinely consider it rather than defend my position.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported openness to correction
questions.developer_notes: Anchor item

questions.text: I can name a time I changed my mind about something important after seeing new evidence.
questions.potential_bias: Recall bias; may reward articulateness over true openness (though scored as agree/disagree, not free text, in this version)
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more accessible evidence of revising behavior
questions.developer_notes: Consider free-text companion in a future qualitative version — out of scope here (fully deterministic scoring required)

questions.text: I get uncomfortable when I realize I've been wrong about something.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability push toward disagreeing regardless of true feeling
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more comfort with being wrong
questions.developer_notes: Distinguishes *comfort with error* from *behavioral openness* (C01) — intentionally a separate facet

questions.text: I'd rather admit I don't know something than guess and sound confident.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported epistemic honesty

questions.text: Once I've made up my mind, I rarely go back on it.
questions.potential_bias: Some respondents read this as 'decisiveness,' a valued trait — risk of the item being answered on a different dimension than intended
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more revision-openness
questions.developer_notes: Flag for cognitive interview in pilot per Part 6

questions.text: I actively seek out opinions that challenge my own.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability; behavior claim, may be aspirational rather than actual
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported active engagement with counter-views
questions.developer_notes: Highest difficulty estimate in domain — expect lower endorsement rates

questions.text: Being shown wrong by someone with less experience than me would bother me.
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = less status-linked defensiveness
questions.developer_notes: Especially relevant to hierarchical clinical settings (attending/trainee dynamics)

questions.text: I think it's a sign of strength, not weakness, to say 'I need to look that up.'
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more positive framing of acknowledged limits

questions.text: If a mentor corrected me in front of others, I'd focus on what I could learn from it.
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track core cluster

questions.text: Being corrected in front of others would mostly just embarrass me.
questions.purpose: Reverse restatement of A01's underlying idea
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track IHOR-A01 after reversal
questions.developer_notes: Consistency pair with IHOR-A01

questions.text: I am always right when I'm confident about something.
questions.expected_interpretation: Agreement (raw ≥3) contributes to SD flag, not domain score
questions.developer_notes: Excluded from domain raw score, routed to validity subsystem, same pattern as SAR-A03/ELCA-A03

questions.text: You've been confidently explaining something to a study group, and a classmate politely shows you that you had a key detail wrong. What's most likely to be your actual next move?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for openness to correction in a realistic, mildly embarrassing social moment.

options.label: Thank them and update what I understood.
Feel a bit embarrassed but move on and quietly note it.
Explain why I thought what I thought, even after they clarify.
Feel annoyed they corrected me in front of the group.

questions.potential_bias: Social desirability strongly favors A; behavioral/scenario framing is the main mitigation available in a deterministic, non-adaptive instrument.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more openness to correction under realistic social discomfort.
questions.developer_notes: B scores 4 rather than 5 to acknowledge that mild embarrassment alongside genuine updating is a normal, healthy response — not a lesser one — while still differentiating it from the fully composed response in A. This is a deliberate design decision to avoid punishing normal emotional reactions.

questions.text: Two weeks after confidently giving a friend advice, you realize the advice was based on something that turned out to be inaccurate. What do you do?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for proactive error-correction (distinct from S01's *reactive* correction scenario).

options.label: Let it go — it's probably not a big deal now.
Bring it up with them so they have accurate information.
Feel bad about it privately but not say anything.
Mention it if it comes up naturally, but not go out of my way.

questions.potential_bias: Consequentiality is unspecified (advice could range from trivial to important); respondents may answer based on an assumed severity that varies person to person — noted as a wording limitation for future revision.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more proactive, self-initiated error correction.
questions.developer_notes: This item specifically targets *proactive* disclosure (relevant to error-disclosure professionalism, and thematically linked to ERP domain) — accepted minor secondary loading on ERP, documented per Part 3 §4.

questions.text: I can act on a decision even when I don't have all the information I'd like.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more tolerance for acting under uncertainty
questions.developer_notes: Anchor item

questions.text: Not knowing the full picture makes me anxious.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability push toward disagreeing regardless of true anxiety level; risk of overlap with clinical anxiety framing — worded generally, not clinically, per §1.3 non-diagnostic requirement
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = less uncertainty-distress
questions.developer_notes: Explicitly reviewed against §1.5(3) safety requirement: general 'anxious' wording only, no clinical terms, no frequency/severity scale that could resemble a screening tool

questions.text: I'm comfortable saying 'it depends' or giving a range instead of a single firm answer.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more comfort with conditional/probabilistic answers
questions.developer_notes: Connects to ECPC domain (communicating uncertainty) but scored here as the *comfort*, not the *delivery skill*

questions.text: I'd rather wait for more certainty than make a call I might have to reverse.
questions.potential_bias: Some respondents view 'waiting for certainty' as prudent rather than avoidant — genuine construct ambiguity, flagged for pilot review
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more comfort acting despite reversal risk
questions.developer_notes: Flag for cognitive interview in pilot; risk that this measures risk-tolerance as much as ambiguity-tolerance

questions.text: Situations with no clear right answer are more interesting to me than frustrating.
questions.purpose: Reframing of ambiguity as engaging rather than aversive
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more positive framing of ambiguous situations

questions.text: I need a plan for every scenario before I feel ready to act.
questions.potential_bias: Overlaps conceptually with CSR (conscientiousness); kept here since framing is specifically about *readiness to act under incomplete planning*, not organization itself
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more comfort acting without exhaustive planning
questions.developer_notes: Cross-domain overlap flagged for Part 3 secondary-loading table

questions.text: When a situation is unclear, I look for the most likely explanation rather than needing the certain one.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more comfort with 'most likely' vs. 'certain' reasoning
questions.developer_notes: Conceptually adjacent to SAR-C08; kept distinct as this item is about *satisficing under ambiguity* specifically

questions.text: I find it stressful when a teacher or supervisor gives instructions that leave room for interpretation.
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more comfort with ambiguous instructions

questions.text: I can be at peace with a decision even if I never find out whether it was the 'right' one.
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track core cluster

questions.text: I replay decisions in my head, wishing I'd had more certainty before making them.
questions.purpose: Reverse restatement of A01's underlying idea
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track AMB-A01 after reversal
questions.developer_notes: Consistency pair with AMB-A01

questions.text: I am never bothered by uncertainty, in any situation, ever.
questions.expected_interpretation: Agreement (raw ≥3) contributes to SD flag, not domain score
questions.developer_notes: Excluded from domain raw score, routed to validity subsystem, consistent pattern across domains

questions.text: You're leading a small group project and the instructions from your professor are genuinely ambiguous about what's expected. The deadline is in two days. What do you do?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for acting productively under genuine ambiguity with a real constraint (time pressure).

options.label: Make a reasonable interpretation and move forward, ready to adjust if needed.
Email the professor and wait to hear back before doing much else.
Feel stuck and put off starting until it's clearer.
Ask the professor, but start working on the parts that are clear regardless.

questions.potential_bias: B and D both involve clarifying-seeking, a generally sound strategy — the differentiation is about whether the respondent also acts in the meantime, which is the actual construct target; risk that some readers see B as equally proactive.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more comfort moving forward productively despite unresolved ambiguity.
questions.developer_notes: A scores highest specifically because it reflects *both* tolerance and initiative; D is a close second because it hedges tolerance with information-seeking, which is a reasonable real-world strategy, not a lesser one — scored close together (5 vs 4) to reflect that closeness.

questions.text: A doctor tells a patient (in a case study you're discussing in class) that a test result is 'probably nothing, but we can't be fully sure without more testing.' If you were the patient, how would you feel about that answer?
questions.purpose: Perspective-taking proxy for comfort with probabilistic medical communication, from the patient's-eye view (distinct from the practitioner-facing framing of most other AMB items).

options.label: Frustrated — I'd want a definite answer.
Uneasy, but I'd understand why they can't promise certainty.
I'd want to understand the actual odds involved rather than just 'probably.'
I wouldn't think much of it either way.

questions.potential_bias: Perspective-taking items can be answered from an idealized 'what I should feel' position rather than a genuine gut reaction; accepted limitation of self-report scenario framing.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more comfort with, and desire to actively engage with, probabilistic uncertainty even from the patient seat — used partly to sanity-check that AMB isn't purely about the respondent's *own* tolerance but generalizes to how they'd want uncertainty handled by others.
questions.developer_notes: Intentional secondary loading on ECPC is accepted and documented (Part 3 §4) rather than rewritten to avoid it, since the perspective-taking angle is valuable and a small amount of cross-loading is expected and acceptable per the domain-interaction design in Part 1 §2.

questions.text: When someone's upset, I try to understand their perspective before responding.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported perspective-taking
questions.developer_notes: Anchor item

questions.text: I'm good at explaining complicated things in simple terms.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = higher self-assessed explanatory clarity

questions.text: I sometimes find myself thinking about what I'll say next instead of fully listening.
questions.potential_bias: Fairly honest-inviting wording (normalizes the behavior) reduces some desirability pressure
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more full listening
questions.developer_notes: Deliberately normalized phrasing to reduce social-desirability distortion, per §3 'avoid obvious desirable answers' requirement

questions.text: I notice non-verbal cues, like tone of voice or body language, pretty easily.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = higher self-assessed non-verbal attunement

questions.text: I'd rather focus on the facts of a situation than how someone is feeling about it.
questions.potential_bias: Some respondents may view this as a neutral/positive trait (objectivity) — genuine construct tension, flagged
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more feeling-attentive default
questions.developer_notes: Interacts with SAR domain (2.1) per Part 1 §2.1 interaction note; not treated as a flaw at high SAR, see report logic Part 4

questions.text: I can usually tell when someone doesn't fully understand what I just explained to them.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = higher self-assessed audience-reading skill

questions.text: Talking about difficult topics (like bad news) makes me want to avoid the conversation if I can.
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = less avoidance of hard conversations
questions.developer_notes: Directly relevant to breaking-bad-news competency per Part 1 §2.5 medical relevance

questions.text: I check in to make sure I actually understood someone, rather than assuming I did.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported check-in behavior

questions.text: People have told me I'm easy to talk to.
questions.purpose: Consistency check via external-validation framing, correlates with core cluster
questions.potential_bias: Depends on whether respondent has received such feedback at all — possible false negatives for genuinely empathic but socially untested respondents
questions.expected_interpretation: Should loosely track core cluster; interpret divergence cautiously
questions.developer_notes: Weakest consistency-pair candidate in domain — noted for pilot review

questions.text: Honestly, I don't think people find me that easy to open up to.
questions.purpose: Reverse restatement of A01's underlying idea
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track ECPC-A01 after reversal
questions.developer_notes: Consistency pair with ECPC-A01

questions.text: I always know exactly how everyone around me is feeling.
questions.expected_interpretation: Agreement (raw ≥3) contributes to SD flag, not domain score
questions.developer_notes: Excluded from domain raw score, routed to validity subsystem

questions.text: A classmate is venting to you about a stressful situation. They don't ask for advice. What are you most likely to actually do?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for listening-first vs. fixing-first communication default.

options.label: Listen and ask questions about how they're feeling before offering anything.
Listen for a bit, then offer some suggestions to help.
Jump in with suggestions since that's usually what's actually useful.
Listen, and just ask if they want advice or just want to vent.

questions.potential_bias: B is a very common, reasonable real-world response and may be under-scored relative to its actual social validity; kept at 3 rather than lower to reflect that it's a legitimate middle-ground, not a poor choice.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more listening-first, consent-checking communication default.
questions.developer_notes: A and D both score 5 as functionally equivalent 'listens first' responses — D is arguably the most patient-centered ideal (explicitly checks what's wanted) but is not weighted higher to avoid over-rewarding a single 'correct-sounding' script.

questions.text: You have to tell someone news they won't want to hear (e.g., a group project grade came back lower than expected and it affects everyone, including a teammate who worked hard). How do you approach it?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for delivering unwelcome information, thematically linked to breaking-bad-news competency.

options.label: Send a message so I don't have to see their reaction.
Tell them directly but plan what to say so it lands gently.
Wait for them to ask, rather than bring it up myself.
Tell them quickly and move on to solutions right away.

questions.potential_bias: A may be under-selected due to obvious social undesirability even if realistically common (avoidance via text) — expect a floor effect on this option; retained anyway since it's a meaningful behavioral anchor.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more thoughtful, direct approach to delivering unwelcome information.
questions.developer_notes: D scores above A/C but below B to reflect that 'quick and solution-focused' is a real, not-uncommon style that has some merit (efficiency) but is scored lower than B's combination of directness and emotional attunement, consistent with the domain's patient-centeredness framing.

questions.text: If I made a mistake that affected someone, I would tell them even if I could get away with not saying anything.
questions.potential_bias: Very high desirability — this whole domain carries the highest social-desirability risk in the bank, per Part 1 §2.6
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported disclosure honesty
questions.developer_notes: Flagged domain-wide for widest interpretive error margin, per Part 1 §2.6

questions.text: I take confidentiality seriously even when it would be easy to share something.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported confidentiality discipline

questions.text: If I noticed a friend cutting corners in a way that could hurt someone, I'd say something even if it strained the friendship.
questions.purpose: Willingness to act on ethical concern despite social cost
questions.potential_bias: High desirability; also genuinely difficult hypothetical, may not predict real behavior
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported willingness to act despite social cost
questions.developer_notes: Acknowledge in report language that hypothetical endorsement often exceeds real-world follow-through (general finding in ethics-vignette research, used as background context, not a specific citation)

questions.text: Small dishonest shortcuts (like fudging a detail on paperwork) don't really bother me if no one gets hurt.
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = less tolerance for minor dishonesty

questions.text: I think it's important to disclose a conflict of interest even if it might make me look bad.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported disclosure willingness

questions.text: I've bent the truth a little to avoid an awkward conversation before.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability push toward disagreeing despite common, near-universal behavior — item deliberately normalizes a common behavior to fight ceiling effects
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution, but **expect and accept a lower ceiling** — near-universal honest endorsement of 'yes, sometimes' is anticipated and not treated as a validity concern by itself
questions.developer_notes: Explicitly excluded from the straight-line/extreme-response validity flags — near-universal moderate agreement here is expected true signal, not a red flag (Part 3 §5.2 exception list)

questions.text: I follow rules even when I'm confident no one would notice if I didn't.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported rule-following under low accountability

questions.text: If I disagreed with a decision my team made, I'd raise it through the proper channels rather than work around it quietly.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported use of proper channels

questions.text: I'd rather take the blame myself than let someone else take it for my mistake.
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track core cluster
questions.developer_notes: Domain-wide desirability caveat applies especially strongly here

questions.text: If I could avoid taking blame for something without anyone finding out, I probably would.
questions.purpose: Reverse restatement of A01's underlying idea
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track ERP-A01 after reversal
questions.developer_notes: Consistency pair with ERP-A01; expect the largest forward/reverse gap in the bank given desirability intensity — noted for pilot calibration

questions.text: I have never once, in my entire life, told even a small white lie.
questions.potential_bias: Very high — designed to catch over-claiming; expect this to be the single most-flagging SD item in the bank
questions.expected_interpretation: Agreement (raw ≥3) contributes to SD flag, not domain score, with **elevated weight in the SD index** (see Part 3 §5.4) given how norm-violating true agreement would be
questions.developer_notes: Excluded from domain raw score, routed to validity subsystem with elevated SD weighting specifically for this item

questions.text: You're working on a group assignment and realize you made an error that, if left uncorrected, would slightly inflate your team's grade. No one else has noticed. What do you do?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for error disclosure under low external accountability and real personal cost (grade).

options.label: Say nothing — it's a small error and unlikely to matter.
Fix it quietly without drawing attention to it.
Tell my teammates and the instructor so it can be properly corrected.
Tell my teammates but not the instructor.

questions.potential_bias: Highest social-desirability pull in the entire scenario set (C is the 'obviously correct' answer) — this is accepted as an unavoidable feature of ethics scenario items and is exactly why ERP carries the widest confidence margin in reporting (Part 1 §2.6, Part 4).
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more proactive, transparent error correction under low accountability.
questions.developer_notes: B is scored meaningfully above A (quiet self-correction is real integrity, just not full transparency) and meaningfully below C/D — a three-tier structure rather than binary right/wrong, matching the 'avoid obvious desirable answers' requirement as much as an ethics item reasonably can.

questions.text: A close friend asks you to cover for them about where they were last night — nothing illegal or dangerous, just something they don't want a family member to know. What do you do?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for honesty under interpersonal/loyalty pressure, deliberately a low-stakes, relatable dilemma (not a medical scenario) so it reads as a genuine values question rather than a 'test.'

options.label: Cover for them — loyalty to a friend matters more here.
Tell them I'm not comfortable lying, but I won't volunteer the information either.
Refuse and tell them they should be honest with their family themselves.
Cover for them without hesitation — it's harmless.

questions.potential_bias: Reasonable people genuinely disagree about small social lies for a friend — this item has the most legitimate value-pluralism of any in the bank, which is why no option scores a full 5; treat divergent answers here as differences in values, not skill.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more consistent honesty orientation even under loyalty pressure, without treating the 'harmless white lie' question as a clear-cut ethics failure.
questions.developer_notes: Deliberately no 5-point option in this item — a design decision documented here — because the underlying dilemma is genuinely contested territory (unlike ERP-S01's clearer-cut case), and manufacturing a 'perfect' answer would misrepresent the ethics literature's own acknowledgment of reasonable disagreement in low-stakes loyalty dilemmas.

questions.text: I usually finish tasks well before the deadline.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported timeliness
questions.developer_notes: Anchor item

questions.text: I keep track of my commitments without needing frequent reminders.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported organizational independence

questions.text: I sometimes start things with enthusiasm but lose steam before finishing.
questions.potential_bias: Fairly honest-inviting wording reduces desirability pressure somewhat
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = more consistent follow-through
questions.developer_notes: Deliberately normalized phrasing per 'avoid obvious desirable answers,' same technique as ECPC-C03

questions.text: I'd rather do a task well and a little late than rush and turn it in on time but sloppy.
questions.potential_bias: Genuine values tradeoff, not a pure conscientiousness signal — flagged
questions.expected_interpretation: Ambiguous; scored as a mild positive but with the lowest discrimination estimate in the domain, reflecting genuine construct ambiguity
questions.developer_notes: Candidate for removal or rewording after pilot data — kept for completeness per 13-item allocation, flagged for review in Part 6

questions.text: I keep a system (calendar, list, app, notebook) to track what I need to do.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability; behavioral claim easy to over-report
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported use of organizational systems

questions.text: Deadlines sneak up on me more often than I'd like to admit.
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = fewer planning gaps

questions.text: Once I commit to something, I follow through even when it stops being convenient.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported reliability under inconvenience
questions.developer_notes: Strong anchor item candidate

questions.text: I can push through a boring or tedious task without needing much external motivation.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported self-motivation under tedium

questions.text: People can count on me to do what I said I'd do.
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track core cluster
questions.developer_notes: Same 'may not have received explicit feedback' caveat as ECPC-A01

questions.text: I've let people down by not following through on something I said I'd do.
questions.purpose: Reverse restatement of A01's underlying idea, honestly-worded
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track CSR-A01 after reversal
questions.developer_notes: Consistency pair with CSR-A01

questions.text: I have never once procrastinated or missed a self-imposed deadline.
questions.expected_interpretation: Agreement (raw ≥3) contributes to SD flag, not domain score
questions.developer_notes: Excluded from domain raw score, routed to validity subsystem

questions.text: You have three things due the same week: a paper, a group project part, and something you volunteered for. You're realizing you're behind on all three. What's most like your actual pattern?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for self-regulation under real competing-deadline pressure.

options.label: Triage — figure out what actually needs to happen first and work through it in order.
Work on whichever one feels most urgent in the moment, even if it's not the most important.
Feel overwhelmed and lose time deciding where to start.
Push through all three at once, even if none of them get my best work.

questions.potential_bias: 'Most like your actual pattern' framing invites idealized self-report over true recalled behavior; scenario framing mitigates but doesn't eliminate this.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more effective self-regulation and prioritization under competing deadlines.
questions.developer_notes: B is scored meaningfully above C/D as a common and not-unreasonable real-world pattern, even though it's not the 'ideal' (A) — avoids collapsing all non-ideal answers into equally low scores, per 'avoid obvious desirable answers' and to give the scale real range.

questions.text: You committed to a study group but you're exhausted and would rather skip it tonight, even though it's not truly an emergency. What do you actually tend to do in situations like this?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for follow-through under low-stakes inconvenience (distinct from S01's high-stakes multi-deadline pressure).

options.label: Go anyway — I said I would.
Go, but show up a bit late or leave early.
Skip it, but let the group know ahead of time.
Skip it without saying anything, and figure I'll explain later if it comes up.

questions.potential_bias: Low — situation is realistic and low-stakes enough to reduce desirability distortion relative to higher-stakes items elsewhere in the bank.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = more consistent follow-through even under minor personal inconvenience.
questions.developer_notes: C is scored reasonably (3, not 1) to avoid punishing honest, communicated changes of plan — this domain should not implicitly define 'conscientious' as 'never changes plans,' only as 'follows through or communicates clearly when not.'

questions.text: When something doesn't go my way, I usually bounce back within a few days.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability; also genuinely variable by setback severity, which the item doesn't specify
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported recovery speed
questions.developer_notes: Anchor item; severity-ambiguity noted, accepted as a general item rather than tied to one specific scenario

questions.text: I have a few go-to strategies I use when I'm stressed (like exercise, talking to someone, or taking a break).
questions.potential_bias: Desirability; behavioral-inventory framing reduces some pull relative to affect-based items
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported coping-strategy repertoire
questions.developer_notes: Deliberately strategy-focused per Part 1 §2.8's emphasis on actionable, learnable coping over trait affect

questions.text: A bad grade or a harsh piece of feedback can put me in a bad mood for a long time.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability push toward disagreeing; wording deliberately avoids clinical terms (e.g., no 'depressed,' no duration/frequency criteria that would resemble a screening item)
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = shorter-lived negative affect response
questions.developer_notes: Explicitly reviewed for clinical-screening resemblance and cleared — no diagnostic language, no severity/frequency criteria

questions.text: If I fail at something, I try to figure out what I can learn from it.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported growth-oriented failure response

questions.text: When I'm struggling, I'm comfortable reaching out to someone for support.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability; help-seeking stigma may suppress honest reporting in the *low* direction more than usual
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported comfort with help-seeking
questions.developer_notes: Framed explicitly as a strength, not a deficit, per Part 1 §2.8's construct definition — avoid any report language that treats low scores here as 'weakness,' see Part 4 tone requirements

questions.text: I tend to expect the worst when something goes wrong, even before I know the details.
questions.potential_bias: Desirability push toward disagreeing; borders on clinical language ('catastrophizing' is a common CBT term) — item itself uses plain language, not the clinical term, to stay in self-report/coping territory rather than screening territory
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = less catastrophizing tendency
questions.developer_notes: Reviewed against §1.5(3); plain-language wording retained specifically to avoid clinical-screening resemblance

questions.text: I can keep functioning reasonably well even during a genuinely stressful stretch (exams, a hard rotation, etc.).
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher = more self-reported functioning under sustained stress
questions.developer_notes: Directly ties to Part 1 §2.8 medical relevance (sustaining engagement through a difficult rotation)

questions.text: Setbacks make me want to give up on the bigger goal, at least for a while.
questions.expected_interpretation: Lower raw agreement = higher contribution = less goal-disengagement under setback
questions.developer_notes: Reviewed against §1.5(3): 'give up... for a while' is deliberately non-clinical, non-permanent phrasing — does not resemble hopelessness/suicidality screening language, which this instrument must never approximate

questions.text: Looking back, I've gotten through hard periods before and come out okay.
questions.purpose: Consistency check with RST-C01/C07 cluster, retrospective framing
questions.potential_bias: Desirability; retrospective framing may not predict future coping
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track core cluster

questions.text: I worry that if things got really hard, I wouldn't be able to cope.
questions.purpose: Reverse restatement of A01's underlying idea
questions.potential_bias: Desirability push toward disagreeing; reviewed against §1.5(3), phrasing kept general/hypothetical rather than about current state, to avoid resembling a present-tense distress screen
questions.expected_interpretation: Should track RST-A01 after reversal
questions.developer_notes: Consistency pair with RST-A01; explicitly reviewed for crisis-screening resemblance and cleared given hypothetical, non-present-tense framing

questions.text: Nothing ever really stresses me out, no matter what happens.
questions.expected_interpretation: Agreement (raw ≥3) contributes to SD flag, not domain score
questions.developer_notes: Excluded from domain raw score, routed to validity subsystem

questions.text: You worked hard on something (an exam, an application, a project) and it didn't go the way you hoped. What's most like your actual pattern over the next few days?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for recovery trajectory after a real setback, in generic (not domain-specific) framing to keep it broadly relatable.

options.label: I feel it for a bit, then start thinking about what's next.
It stays with me for a while, but I still keep up with my regular routine.
It's hard to focus on much else for a while.
I try to talk to someone about it, which helps me move forward.

questions.potential_bias: Same recall-idealization limitation noted throughout the scenario set; also, severity of 'didn't go the way you hoped' is left deliberately unspecified/generic, which trades some precision for broad applicability across very different respondent situations.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = healthier, more functional recovery trajectory after a real setback.
questions.developer_notes: C is not scored as low as the 'worst' possible response (kept at 2, not 1) — a period of genuinely reduced focus after a real setback is normal and expected, and scoring it near-zero would pathologize a normal reaction, inconsistent with the domain's non-clinical framing (§1.3, §2.8).

questions.text: You're in the middle of a demanding stretch (finals, a heavy work period, etc.) and someone asks how you're managing. What's closest to your honest answer?
questions.purpose: Behavioral proxy for functioning-under-sustained-stress, self-reported in the moment rather than retrospectively (distinct from S01's after-the-fact framing).

options.label: It's a lot, but I'm managing okay.
I'm keeping up, but it's taking a toll.
Honestly, I'm barely keeping my head above water.
I've had to let some things slide to keep up with the rest.

questions.potential_bias: C is worded close to a genuine distress signal by design (this domain's construct legitimately includes 'currently struggling') — this is why the safety-routing logic in Part 3 §5 must check RST-S02 (and RST-C03/C06/C08) responses as part of the pre-scoring safety gate, not just the free-text fields.
questions.expected_interpretation: Higher score = better self-reported functioning under current sustained stress.
questions.developer_notes: **This item is explicitly flagged for the safety-routing table in Part 3** — a response of C, especially in combination with low scores on RST-C03/C05/C08, should be evaluated by the pre-scoring safety gate (§1.5(3)) before the assessment proceeds to a normal report, per the crisis-handling requirement established in Part 1.
